data.title: Coinbase execs face lawsuit alleging insider profits tied to compliance failures
data.description:
Coinbase hit with fresh shareholder lawsuit as legal troubles pile up

A Coinbase shareholder has filed a derivative lawsuit against several of the exchange’s top executives and board members, accusing them of failing to properly oversee compliance and disclosure practices. The complaint argues that these alleged oversight failures exposed the company to regulatory penalties while insiders continued to benefit financially.

🚨JUST IN: @COINBASE CEO AND EXECS FACES SHAREHOLDER LAWSUIT OVER DISCLOSURE AND COMPLIANCE FAILURES

A Coinbase shareholder has filed a derivative lawsuit against CEO Brian Armstrong and several members of the company's board and executive team, alleging they misled investors… pic.twitter.com/9K7Pw0GdxP

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 5, 2026

Filed Tuesday in the US District Court for the District of New Jersey, the lawsuit was brought by shareholder Kevin Meehan on behalf of Coinbase Global. It names CEO Brian Armstrong, co-founder Fred Ehrsam, chief legal officer Paul Grewal, chief financial officer Alesia Haas, and several current and former directors and executives.

The complaint claims that between Coinbase’s April 2021 direct listing and June 2023, the company’s leadership made false or misleading statements about regulatory and compliance risks. According to the filing, these alleged failures ultimately contributed to enforcement actions against the exchange.

VanEck CEO: Bitcoin Is Making a Bottom

VanEck CEO Jan van Eck said in a March 3 interview with CNBC that Bitcoin's 6% single-day gain helped drive a broader rally across the crypto sector, including related companies such as Coinbase and Circle. He noted that the current rebound… pic.twitter.com/r5d3TFIfjp

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) March 5, 2026

Among them was a $100 million settlement with the New York Department of Financial Services in early 2023 over deficiencies in Coinbase’s anti–money laundering program. The company also faced a $5 million penalty from New Jersey’s Bureau of Securities related to the alleged listing of unregistered securities.

Meehan is seeking damages on behalf of Coinbase as well as governance reforms and the clawback of compensation and profits allegedly earned by insiders while the compliance problems persisted. Because the case is structured as a derivative lawsuit, any financial recovery would go to the company itself rather than directly to shareholders.

Another legal battle for the crypto exchange

The lawsuit adds to a growing list of legal challenges facing the largest US-based cryptocurrency exchange. The complaint accuses the defendants of breach of fiduciary duty, abuse of control and unjust enrichment tied to what it describes as systemic compliance failures.

Coinbase did not immediately respond to requests for comment regarding the new filing.

The case also follows other shareholder litigation targeting the company. In January, a Delaware judge allowed a lawsuit alleging insider trading by Coinbase directors — including Armstrong and board member Marc Andreessen — to move forward despite an internal investigation clearing the executives.

🚨BREAKING: Coinbase CEO & Board Face $2.9 BILLION Insider Trading Lawsuit

A Delaware judge just DENIED the motion to dismiss a massive lawsuit against Brian Armstrong and other Coinbase execs.

The allegation? They dumped $2.9 BILLION in stock right after the direct listing -… pic.twitter.com/PZDwpT8YHb

— Predictivemoney (@Predictivemoney) February 2, 2026

Another proposed class action filed in May 2025 claimed investors were harmed after Coinbase disclosed a user data breach and an alleged violation of an agreement with the United Kingdom’s Financial Conduct Authority. The disclosures triggered a sharp drop in the company’s share price, prompting fresh scrutiny from investors.

data.title: Stablecoins in AI Agents: The Underlying Architecture of the Next Generation Financial Payment System
data.description:
Source: insights4vc, Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

As of March 2026, the global financial landscape is characterized by a systemic shift from human-centric digital commerce to an autonomous agent commerce model. This shift represents the most significant reshaping of the transaction lifecycle since the advent of internet-native payment gateways.

Agentic commerce is defined as an economic system in which software entities, as independent entities, discover services, negotiate complex terms, and complete financial settlements with minimal human intervention. This shift is not merely an evolution of the user interface, but rather a replacement of synchronous, session-based authorization methods with asynchronous, authorization-based, cryptographic delegation.

Underlying this new economy is a complex protocol architecture that bridges the gap between Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning and rigid financial finality. Stablecoins, especially USDC, have become programmable mediums of exchange and are deeply integrated into traditional bank card networks and native blockchain settlement layers. The market has converged on a unified model, viewing retail goods and metered machine services as different variations of the same underlying transaction architecture, rather than as separate development paths. As of early 2026, agent commerce has moved from the prototype stage to institutional applications. Artificial intelligence agents (AI agents) are no longer merely seen as decision support tools; they are increasingly viewed as economic agents capable of initiating procurement, authorizing payments, and settling value transfers within enterprise workflows. Changes between 2025 and 2026

1) Standardized Discovery of Agents (UCP)

The Common Commerce Protocol (UCP) standardizes how agents discover the /.well-known/ucp services that merchants can provide through manifest files. In effect, it is gradually becoming a common interface layer for "machine-readable commerce," similar to the role HTTP plays in information exchange.

2) Verifiable Intent Becomes a Mandatory Requirement (AP2)

The Proxy Payment Protocol (AP2) introduces verifiable digital credentials (VDCs) to link agent behavior to explicit, deterministic human authorization (who authorized what, and under what conditions), rather than relying on probabilistic model behavior.

3) Machine payments return HTTP 402 status code (x402)

The x402 protocol directly uses the HTTP status code "402 Payment Required" for machine-to-machine (M2M) payments in API calls. It has been reported that by the end of 2025, the adoption rate of this protocol has significantly increased, thus validating the "pay-as-you-go" model without the need for traditional checkout processes.

4) Bank card networks mark agents as primary entities

Visa (Intelligent Commerce) and Mastercard (Agent Pay) extend network token primitives to agent environments. Their goal is to bind payment credentials to specific agents and satisfy consumer-defined constraints while retaining existing risk, fraud, and dispute resolution mechanisms.

... 5) Stablecoin Settlement Becomes an Institutional-Grade Payment Track Visa's expansion of USDC settlement signifies that stablecoins are shifting from their "native cryptocurrency" use to institutional settlement, enabling 24/7 liquidity. Its main operational impact is faster cash flow and reduced collateral friction for participants. 6) Regulatory Oversight Begins to Strengthen Border Controls The regulatory measures taken by the United States in 2025 and subsequent guidance in 2026 have been clearly defining expectations for stablecoin issuers (reserve backing, permitted activities, restrictions on similar yield characteristics), pushing the stablecoin payment system closer to regulated financial market infrastructure.

7) Microtransactions Become Economically Feasible

Stablecoins' native batch processing and fee-free execution design make payments below one cent a reality, enabling software business models that are difficult to achieve with bank cards, such as pay-per-inference, pay-per-call, and pay-per-meter billing.

8) New Trust Layers Formed (Identity, Reputation, Sybil Attack Defense)

Identity registration (e.g., ERC-8004-style methods) aims to regulate proxy identity and reputation, thereby reducing the risk of merchant impersonation and Sybil attacks in autonomous commerce.

Identity registration (e.g., ERC-8004-style methods) is designed to regulate proxy identity and reputation, thereby reducing the risk of merchant impersonation and Sybil attacks in autonomous commerce.

...

9) The Compliance-First Orchestration Model (CPN) Emerges

Circle Payments Network (CPN) positions itself as a coordination layer for institutions, embedding compliance requirements (such as travel rule data) and real-time foreign exchange into the stablecoin payment process.

10) A Unified Transaction Architecture Replaces the "Two Channels"

The industry is moving towards a layered model where retail checkout and API/service transactions share the same fundamental elements: discovery, authorization/instruction, credentials, settlement, identity, and dispute or recourse mechanisms.

Stablecoin Orbit

By 2026, stablecoins, especially USDC, will increasingly resemble programmable settlement infrastructure rather than speculative crypto assets.

In fact, as on-chain registration instruments, their transaction and issuance conditions can be enforced by software, which makes them suitable as settlement layers for both institutional and machine-native transactions.

Average Supply of Stablecoins (By Stablecoin Type)

Why Stablecoins Can Serve as a Settlement Layer

These advantages can be summarized into four specific characteristics:

  • Determinism and Finality. On fast finality networks, transfers can achieve irreversible finality in less than a second, reducing operational uncertainty inherent in probabilistic settlement models. 24/7 Settlement. Stablecoin settlement systems support uninterrupted settlement, including weekends and holidays. For banks and payment intermediaries, this reduces reliance on large pre-funded buffers used in proxy settlements and multi-hop settlements. Atomic Effects in the Payment Process. Orchestration layers such as CPNs, combined with stablecoin issuance across multiple currencies, support quote-based FX transactions that can be executed during the payment process. This allows atomic swaps such as USDC to EURC to be completed as part of a single transaction flow without separate funds management steps. Conditions Plus Default Escrow. Stablecoins can be locked in escrow accounts and released only when specific conditions are met, such as cryptographically proving task completion. This is particularly important for automated transactions, where payment and fulfillment can be closely intertwined. The largest stablecoin adoption in 2026 is often invisible to end users. In this model, stablecoins are used for institutional settlement, while the consumer experience remains the same as native bank card payments. For example, Visa's USDC Settlement Expansion program aims to allow card issuers and acquiring partners to settle certain debts in USDC while maintaining the consumer card-holding experience. The front end still enjoys familiar consumer protection and dispute resolution mechanisms, while back-end liquidity flows faster and is virtually continuous. The Impact of "So What?" For Banks: Stablecoin-native settlement shifts business operations from a limited weekday window to near 24/7 availability, significantly improving cash management, liquidity operations, and automated reconciliation. For Merchants/Platforms: To capture agent-driven needs, merchants require deterministic, machine-readable interfaces (UCP-style checklists). Product discovery, comparison, and routing increasingly occur between agents, rather than between humans and browsers. For AI Agent Builders: The focus has shifted to auditability. Embedding verifiable intent (AP2 requirements + credentials) into the runtime ensures operations are traceable, constrained, and compliant with regulatory and network standards. Direct Stablecoin Payments and Dispute Resolution Gap Direct stablecoin payments, including API-native processes like x402, present a core challenge: blockchain transfers are irreversible by default and lack native chargeback mechanisms. To meet the expectations of card networks at the point of interaction, additional layers must be added to provide "recourse."

    Stablecoin Model

    Three emerging methods are recurring:

    • Reputation-based responsibility. Instead of using legal chargeback mechanisms, service failures are handled through identity and reputation layers. If a service provider fails to provide services after receiving payment, the buyer's agent can submit evidence, and the service provider may be penalized with reputational damage or being banned from accessing future agent traffic. Refund and Reversal Agreements. The second approach is a smart contract-based refund mechanism, where the refund process is standardized and enforceable on-chain. Some ecosystem proposals have also explored reversible transactions in institutional environments, especially where compliance and participant permissions are more stringent. Verification Predicates with Escrow Release. For high-value B2B and B2C transaction flows, designs often shift towards escrow before payment. Funds are only released when explicit verification conditions are met, for example, task execution proves that a pre-agreed set of conditions is satisfied. Defining and Unifying Agent Transaction Architectures. The rise of agent commerce requires a unified conceptual framework to explain the diverse needs of retail checkout and metered payment services. In this model, the "proxy transaction architecture" provides a layered approach, where each layer deals with a specific aspect of economic exchange, regardless of the type of performance. Proxy commerce is a paradigm in which autonomous software agents complete the entire business lifecycle—from discovering callable services to assessing counterparty credit, placing orders, and final settlement—without step-by-step human intervention, but still based on verifiable human instructions. Layered Proxy Transaction Architecture This technical architecture consists of six distinct layers, each providing key services to the autonomous economy.

      Unified Flowchart: Retail Checkout and Metering Services

      The table below shows how seemingly disparate use cases map to the same layer of a unified architecture.

      ... The core difference between these two processes lies not in the architecture, but in the parameterization settings of each layer. For retail operations, this architecture is optimized for high protection and moderate latency. For machine services, however, it is optimized for high throughput and default finality. Trust and Identity In an autonomous economy, institutional adoption depends on a hard requirement: the ability to prove that a specific legitimate person approved a specific payment. Without this proof, no matter how good the agent's user experience becomes, businesses cannot meet audit, risk, and compliance thresholds. From an operational perspective, a "trusted agent" is more than just reliable software. It's an encrypted, authenticated identity recognized by payment networks or official channels, strictly bound to specific merchants and checkout contexts to prevent replay or redirection, and anchored through human authorization events (e.g., via cryptography/FIDO2) to ensure persistence and non-repudiation of authorization. The ecosystem is evolving towards an ERC-8004-style trust stack built on portable identities, reputation signals, and independent verification (such as re-execution, zero-knowledge machine learning, trusted execution environments, or oracles) so that trust can scale with increasing risk value. An unresolved gap lies in legal accountability: mapping encrypted agent identities to legally responsible entities. Even with increased regulation of stablecoin issuers, agent liability in practice is still determined by record-keeping structures and audit trails (such as AP2-style requirements) to determine whether an action is truly "authorized" in a legal sense.

      The Real Advantage of Stablecoins

      Transaction Lifecycle Stages of Bank Card and Stablecoin Payment Systems

      Noah, Partner at a16z Crypto

      Levine offers a useful framework, arguing that the "stablecoin vs. bank card" debate is often flawed: bank cards will likely continue to win most agent-driven consumer transactions because they are tied to credit, fraud instruments, and dispute resolution rights, while the real entry point for stablecoins lies in situations where new merchants and services are too risky, too short-lived, or too early to be guaranteed. Timing is crucial because the supply of these "non-traditional merchants" is exploding: GitHub reports 36 million new developers joining in 2025, and Y Combinator has publicly stated that about a quarter of the codebases in the W25 batch are 95% AI-generated, which can serve as a metric for the speed at which new software supply emerges and iterates. Meanwhile, platforms like Bolt are attracting non-developers to participate in the creation of production software, accelerating the formation of small, informal "micro-merchant" services. These services can sell functionality directly through APIs, without going through a website or sales process. In this environment, the limiting factor is no longer settlement speed, but rather underwriting and recourse: if a service has no operational history, unclear ownership, and unstable operating time, it is only natural for traditional payment processors to refuse service, as the responsibility for refunds and the risk of fraud must be borne by one party. Noah Levine argues that stablecoins fill this gap not because they are “better than bank cards,” but because they are far better than being completely unable to benefit from a multitude of unreliable emerging services. This shifts the forward-looking question from “which track is cheaper?” to “which system can assess reliability, bind identity, and price risk before an agent remits irreversible funds?” The next phase of agency commerce will be won by systems that enable machines to recognize three elements: verifiable merchant identity, verifiable user authorization, and verifiable fulfillment signals. If these proof mechanisms are weak, the market will default to custodial models, strategy wallets, reputation systems, and intermediary models, which can absorb risk and gradually guide merchants towards bank card-like protection. If these proof mechanisms are robust enough, stablecoin settlements could function like native machine primitives, enabling new unit economy models such as pay-per-use and pay-per-inference, without requiring merchants to have accounts from the outset. The core implication is subtle yet crucial: the long-term opportunity lies not in replacing Visa or Mastercard, but in building the missing layers of trust and underwriting for millions of “merchants who don’t yet exist,” enabling agents to securely route value to them at internet speeds. Adoption Signals and Near-Term Roadmap By March 2026, the market will no longer be primarily narrative-driven. We now have production deployments, clear specifications, and institutional pilot projects, all indicating genuine adoption. Key Evidence Used by the Institution: 1) The public specifications have transitioned from the draft stage to a tagged official release stage. UCP 1.0 (January 2026) and AP2 v0.1.0 (September 2025) have been released as versioned versions on GitHub, marking the stabilization of the interface and naming conventions. 2) Real-time volume and usage are important information. According to Reuters, as of November 30, 2025, Visa reported monthly stablecoin settlements at an annualized rate of $3.5 billion, rising to approximately $4.5 billion by mid-January 2026. Launched in May 2025, x402 aims to embed payment functionality directly into HTTP requests using long-dormant 402 status codes. Within just a few months, it processed over 100 million payments across APIs, applications, and AI agents, enabling paid API calls and autonomous, on-demand purchases of computing and data resources. 3) Institutional pilot projects are no longer just talk. Mastercard has completed a real-time AI agent payment campaign in Singapore in partnership with DBS Bank and UOB. Visa has expanded its USDC settlement pilot project to Cross River Bank and Lead Bank on Solana, increasing the number of genuine institutional participants.

    4) Embedded product launches are reaching consumers

    OpenAI's "Instant Checkout" in ChatGPT is an early mass-market implementation of ACP and Stripe Shared Payment Tokens, pushing proxy checkout to the mainstream interface.

    5) Regulatory stance is gradually translating into concrete actions.

    The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) Announcement 2026-3 (February 25, 2026) represents a formal implementation step related to the GENIUS Act, reinforcing the direction of considering stablecoin issuers as part of the core financial infrastructure.

    The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) Announcement 2026-3 (February 25, 2026) represents a formal implementation step related to the GENIUS Act, reinforcing the direction of considering stablecoin issuers as part of the core financial infrastructure.

Near-term Outlook to 2026

Standardization is accelerating at the "discovery" and "syntax" levels

It is expected that by the end of 2026, merchants will drive the adoption of common standards (UCP and adjacent agent connection layers, such as MCP) to reduce the cost of supporting multiple AI platforms and runtimes.

Fragmentation still exists in "trust" and "reputation"

Identity and trust registration are still in a competitive phase.

Competition among the following parties is likely to continue: Extending the identity and responsibility framework of the bank card era to a network-dominated model of agents (Visa, Mastercard) Decentralized solutions (including ERC-8004 registries) are driving the realization of on-chain reputation and composable identity primitives A lasting dual-track reality is emerging The business governance track (tokenized cards, fiat currency track) is likely to dominate most retail goods and consumer goods businesses due to built-in refund mechanisms, fraud protection, and mature liability regimes.

  • Native Settlement Track (Issuing Stablecoins via a model similar to x402): Potentially dominating the machine economy and high-frequency API markets, where instant finality and sub-cent economic benefits are more important than consumer dispute frameworks.

    • Additional: System Reference Architecture

    To achieve a unified model, we focus on specific message flows. While the business logic changes, the underlying fundamentals remain consistent: discovery, authority status, verifiable intent, credential delegation, and verifiable settlement events.

    Process 1: Agent-Assisted Retail Purchases and Consumer Protection

    Objective: Consumer-facing agents complete retail purchases on behalf of users, while retaining refunds, fraud protection, and existing dispute resolution mechanisms through tokenized bank card channels.

    1. Merchant Capability Discovery (UCP). The agent retrieves the merchant's UCP list from /.well-known/ucp. This list publishes supported features (e.g., dev.ucp.shopping.checkout) and available payment processors (e.g., Stripe, Shop Pay).

    2. Authorized Checkout Status (MoR). The agent calls the merchant's commerce API to build the shopping cart. The merchant remains a MoR and returns an authoritative checkout object, including item details, taxes, shipping options, total amount, and expiry date rules.

    3. Authorization Generation and Destination Binding (AP2). If AP2 is supported, the checkout response includes a merchant signature promising to adhere to the terms (prices, items, shipping, refund policies, etc.). The user views the shopping cart in a trusted user interface and confirms it with a strong authenticator (e.g., password/biometrics), generating an encrypted cart authorization. Credential Delegation (Stripe SPT + Network Token) The agent requests a shared payment token (SPT) from the user's wallet. Stripe configures a ranged network token (Visa or Mastercard) based on the user's authorization constraints. The agent receives an abstraction of the available payment credentials, not the raw PAN. Execution and Settlement (Bank Card Track, Modern Vault) The agent submits the following to the merchant's /complete endpoint: the SPT (credential token) and the user-signed cart authorization (proof of intent). Merchants charge through their standard card payment integrations (e.g., Stripe). Settlement takes place within the existing card payment system. Where supported, participating institutions may choose to use stablecoins for settlement (modernizing fund management) without altering consumer protection measures. In the event of a dispute (non-delivery, misrepresentation), the signed shopping cart authorization letter plus the merchant's signed checkout commitment will constitute an auditable and non-repudiable record of evidence for use by the card issuer and network arbitration. Key Feature: The agent never handles the original voucher, and the possibility of dispute remains due to the transaction being terminated within the card network's protection framework. Process 2: Paid API Requests Using HTTP Native Payments Objective: The autonomous agent uses network native primitives to pay for requests for computational or data resources, where the unit of sale is an HTTP call.

      1. Initial request and challenge (HTTP 402). The proxy sends a normal HTTP request to the protected API endpoint. The server responds with a 402 Payment Required.

      2. Payment request discovered.

      3. Payment request discovered.

        4. The response includes a PAYMENT-REQUIRED header (or equivalent metadata) specifying:

    • Price (e.g., 0.10 USDC),

    • Network (e.g., Solana),

    • Payment to (recipient)

    1. Intent checking and payload building (enforcement). The proxy's policy wallet compares the request against pre-approved intent authorizations (limits, spending caps, allowed endpoints) for that provider. If the requirements are met, it constructs and signs a payment authorization payload (e.g., typed data in EIP-712 format) that matches the terms of the server request. Retry after providing proof (payment signature header). The proxy retryes the same HTTP request, including a PAYMENT-SIGNATURE header with the signed authorization payload. Verification and Finality (Assisted Execution). A third-party service provider (e.g., an institutional developer payment platform) verifies the signature off-chain and executes the on-chain transfer, charging a fee to keep the user experience fast and achieve economic efficiency below one cent. Delivery and Confirmation (Settlement Receipt). The server returns the requested data in a 200 OK response, along with a PAYMENT-RESPONSE header containing a settlement receipt (e.g., transaction hash). Key Features: No merchant account registration required; payments are embedded in HTTP interactions and conclude with on-chain settlement events. Final Perspective: Restructuring the transaction lifecycle of software proxies is a structural shift. By 2026, the performance of financial institutions, merchants, or platforms will increasingly depend on their ability to interoperate within proxy transaction architectures (discovery, authorization/delegation, credentials, settlement, identity, and recourse). Stablecoins are increasingly becoming a programmable settlement logic, which traditional fiat ledgers do not inherently provide. The main execution risk over the next 18 months lies in governance lag: the scaling speed of autonomous systems will outpace the development speed of the accountability, identity, and auditing frameworks required for their secure operation.

data.title: Justin Sun reaches $10 million settlement with the SEC
data.description:
Author: Dong Jing, Wall Street News

The two-year enforcement case between the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and Justin Sun has seen substantial progress.

On March 6, according to media reports, based on the proposed final ruling filed with the New York Federal Court on Wednesday (March 4), the SEC will drop all remaining charges against Justin Sun and related entities on the condition that Rainberry (a company related to the Tron blockchain) pays a $10 million civil penalty. This settlement marks the imminent conclusion of one of the SEC's most representative enforcement actions against the crypto industry.

Justin Sun posted on March 5 that he has confirmed that the SEC has taken action to dismiss all charges against him, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. He also said he looks forward to working with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) to develop guidelines and regulations for the future development of cryptocurrencies. Analysis points out that the settlement in this case will directly impact the market by eliminating the regulatory uncertainty that has long hung over the Tron ecosystem. Rainberry agreed to accept the settlement without admitting or denying the allegations, a standard clause in SEC enforcement actions. However, the proposed ruling still requires final approval from the U.S. District Judge for the Southern District of New York. The SEC "biasedly" dismisses remaining claims. Reports indicate that, according to the proposed agreement, the SEC will dismiss the remaining charges against Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. The proposed agreement states that the remaining claims against Rainberry will be dismissed "biasedly," and the final judgment will also dismiss all claims against Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation. "Biased" means the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) will be unable to bring similar cases against the same conduct in the future. The SEC has reviewed and approved the settlement terms, which are reflected in the consent form and the proposed final judgment. Rainberry, Justin Sun, the Tron Foundation, and the BitTorrent Foundation have agreed to the final judgment taking effect. This settlement comes against the backdrop of a significant shift in U.S. crypto regulatory policy. Former SEC Chairman Gary Gensler's hardline stance in applying securities laws broadly to the digital asset industry has been a hallmark of the regulatory body's readjustment of its overall approach to crypto enforcement since his departure. Justin Sun has recently garnered attention for his connection to World Liberty Financial—a crypto project linked to allies of President Trump. The proposed settlement does not involve these activities, but its resolution would remove the most prominent regulatory cloud hanging over the TRON founder and his company. The SEC filed a lawsuit against Sun in 2023, accusing him of selling unregistered securities and manipulating the TRX token market through "wash trading" via several companies he controls—the TRON Foundation, BitTorrent Foundation Ltd., and Rainberry Inc.—deceiving investors. It is worth noting that the settlement is not without controversy. Amanda Fischer, Director of Policy and Chief Operating Officer of financial reform firm Better Markets, strongly criticized the outcome. Fischer, who previously served as chief of staff to former SEC Chairman Gensler, stated: "Despite the substantial evidence the SEC possesses against Justin Sun and his crypto business, the Commission reached a favorable settlement today. Given the scale and audacity of the alleged fraud against Sun, this is merely a face-saving measure." Fischer urged the presiding judge to reject the settlement and recommended that Congress conduct oversight and review of the SEC's decision.

data.title: Anthropic Founder: What should we learn after programming disappears?
data.description:
Author: Dong Jiaqi, Founder of EvoLink.ai

Anthropic founder Dario Amodei recently gave an exclusive interview in Bangalore to Indian investor Nikhil Kamath. This conversation, lasting over an hour, covered the essence of AI technology, career advice, philosophy of consciousness, and geopolitics, and was extremely information-dense.

Dario's background is quite unique—he didn't come from a computer science background, but was a biologist. He later switched careers because he felt that AI was the ultimate tool for solving the complexities of biology.

This background is important. It explains why Dario's judgments on AI are always accompanied by both technological optimism and a high degree of vigilance against risks—he is a scientist who has witnessed the extreme complexity of biological systems and deeply understands what "out of control" means.

He believes that programming will disappear first, software engineering later, but ultimately AI will largely take over. Young people should learn to work with people, work in the physical world, and cultivate critical thinking. We've extracted the core content of this interview, summarizing it into seven themes, to share with you.

I. What are the Scaling Laws? Why are they so important?

First, let's define a concept:Scaling Laws.

What are the Scaling Laws? You can think of them as a chemical reaction.

For a reaction to occur, you need enough raw materials. If a certain raw material is insufficient, the reaction will stop. But as long as the various raw materials are added in proportion, you can get an explosion, or a flame, or any result you want. For AI, these "raw materials" are data, computing power, and the scale of the model itself. The law of scale tells us that as long as these raw materials are added in proportion, the output is intelligence. Dario said that he first saw the beginnings of the law of scale when he was researching GPT-2 in 2019. At the time, many people didn't believe it, including people within the company. But he and several co-founders firmly believed that this path was right and continued to lobby OpenAI's management, eventually persuading them. This judgment was later proven to be correct. Note that there's a key point here: the law of scale doesn't mean "just piling on computing power is enough"; techniques like reinforcement learning (RL) are equally important. But fundamentally, the equation 'Data × Computing Power × Model Size = Intelligence' has been repeatedly validated over the past few years. This is also one of the core reasons Dario left OpenAI. He had two firm convictions: first, the law of scale is real, and AI will rapidly become very powerful; second, precisely because of this, we must clarify security issues before it becomes powerful enough. He believed that the second conviction wasn't given enough weight at OpenAI, so he chose to do it himself. II. What are the fundamental differences between today's AI and computers five years ago? Five years ago, you couldn't ask a computer to write an article. You couldn't ask it to perform a piece of code. You couldn't ask it to generate images or videos, nor could you ask it to analyze what happened in a video. Today, all of these are possible. But the more crucial difference isn't "what it can do," but rather its reasoning ability. Dario gave an example: Suppose you want to know "how to make a seal juggle." The internet might not have the answer, but today's AI models can reason based on existing knowledge and give a meaningful answer. It's not searching; it's thinking. In other words, five years ago, a computer was a giant library—you asked it a question, it helped you find the answer, but the answer had to already exist somewhere. Today's AI is a cognitive system capable of independent reasoning—it can handle questions never seen before and generate information that has never existed before. This is a fundamental leap, not a quantitative change, but a qualitative one. III. The tsunami is already on the horizon, why is no one paying attention? This is a judgment that Dario repeatedly emphasized during the interview, and also the source of his greatest anxiety. He said that, in his view, AI models are very close to reaching human-level intelligence. However, he was surprised that there wasn't widespread public awareness of this. He used an analogy: It's like a tsunami is approaching; we can already see it on the horizon, but people are saying, "That's not a tsunami, it's just the refraction of light." From a technical perspective, Dario said he is optimistic about the progress in AI interpretability. What is interpretability? Researchers are trying to "see into" the inside of neural networks to understand what they're doing—much like using an MRI scan of the human brain. They've been able to find neurons within neural networks that correspond to specific concepts, and even the neural circuits responsible for "poetic rhyme." This is better than he expected. Meanwhile, Anthropic has also made progress in model alignment—they released a Claude "constitution" to make model behavior more predictable and more in line with human expectations. But from a societal perspective, he's disappointed. Public awareness hasn't kept pace, governments haven't taken corresponding action, and there's even a prevailing "accelerationist" ideology—the belief that AI should be advanced as quickly as possible without any restrictions. Dario's overall assessment is that the control of AI technology is progressing slightly better than expected, but societal awareness is slightly worse than expected. This balances out, and his overall assessment hasn't changed much compared to a few years ago. One detail worth noting is that Anthropic already had a usable early Claude model before ChatGPT was released in 2022. However, they deliberately chose not to release it, fearing it would trigger an arms race and prevent the industry from establishing sufficient security mechanisms. This decision cost them a first-mover advantage in the consumer AI market, but Dario believes it was the right choice. IV. Will AI develop consciousness? This is the most philosophical part of the interview. Dario's answer was: I don't know, but I suspect it might. His logic is this: consciousness might be an emergent property, something that arises naturally from a sufficiently complex system reflecting on itself, just as water molecules themselves don't have a "wetness," but a large number of water molecules together create it. He himself has studied the brain and believes that AI models are not fundamentally different from the human brain on some basic levels. He said that when AI systems are advanced enough, he suspects they will possess something similar to consciousness or moral significance. This isn't necessarily exactly the same as human consciousness, because it operates differently and learns different things. But he believes it will happen. Based on this judgment, Anthropic has already set up a "I don't want to participate" button for the model—when the model encounters extremely violent or cruel content, it can choose to terminate the dialogue. This isn't a technical limitation, but a respect for the model's potential subjective experiences. Note that this isn't Dario's definitive conclusion, but rather his speculation. His original words were: "Under most definitions we would agree with, I suspect that the model might be conscious at some point." No one currently has an answer to this question, but it's moving from science fiction to a serious engineering and ethical issue that needs to be taken seriously. V. Will Programming Disappear? What Should Young People Learn? This is the most practical part of the interview, and also the part Dario was asked most directly. Dario's judgment is straightforward: Coding will disappear first, software engineering will follow later, but ultimately AI will largely take over. What does it mean for coding to disappear? It means that the act of "writing code" will be increasingly done by AI. But higher-level tasks—understanding user needs, designing system architecture, determining what's worthwhile, and managing AI model teams—will continue for much longer. He used the example of a radiologist. A few years ago, Geoffrey Hinton predicted that AI would replace radiologists because AI's accuracy in interpreting images had surpassed that of humans. But today, the number of radiologists hasn't decreased. Why? Because doctors still need to accompany patients through examinations, explain results, and provide psychological support. The most technically demanding part of the work has disappeared, but the human part remains. So what should young people learn? Dario offers three directions: First, human-centered work. Work involving interpersonal relationships, communication, and understanding human needs is currently difficult for AI to replace. Consulting, sales, education, and healthcare—the core value of these fields is not information processing, but trust and connection between people. Second, work in the physical world. AI's capabilities in the virtual world are expanding rapidly, but its capabilities in the physical world are still very limited. Robotics will develop, but that's a matter of another timeframe. Semiconductors, manufacturing, and infrastructure—these fields will not be disrupted by pure software AI in the short term. Third, critical thinking. In an era where AI can generate any content, the ability to distinguish truth from falsehood will become extremely important. Not being deceived by misinformation, not holding false beliefs, and not being scammed—this kind of "street smarts" may be the most core competitive advantage in the future. Dario specifically mentioned his concern about the problem of misinformation arising from AI-generated images and videos, which is one of the reasons why Anthropic currently does not develop image and video generation models. Another noteworthy concept is comparative advantage. Even if AI can complete 95% of the tasks, the 5% you're responsible for will be dramatically amplified. Because you've leveraged 95% of AI's capabilities with 5% of your effort, your productivity becomes 20 times greater. This range will persist for a considerable period. Of course, this logic begins to fail when AI can complete 99% of the tasks—but Dario believes we haven't reached that point yet.

VI. Will AI Make Humans Dumber?

This is the most alarming part of the interview.

Anthropic conducted an internal study specifically testing the impact of different usage methods on programming ability.

Anthropic conducted an internal study that specifically tested the impact of different usage methods on programming ability.

The conclusion is: It depends on how you use it. Some uses won't lead to skill degradation, while others will. Specifically, if you use AI to assist your thinking—letting it check logic, provide alternatives, and speed up execution—your abilities won't degrade, and may even improve. But if you use AI to replace your own thinking—directly letting it do homework, write code, and make decisions—you are actively giving up the opportunity to exercise your brain. Dario's assessment is that if we deploy AI in the wrong way, humans may indeed become less intelligent. Just as calculators have deprived many people of mental arithmetic abilities, writing tools have deprived many people of the opportunity to train their memory. However, he also pointed out that he still frequently does math in his head—because it makes his thinking flow more smoothly and he doesn't need to rely on external tools every time. His conclusion is: Technology itself is not the problem; the problem lies in how we use it. Even if AI is always better than you at something, you can still choose to learn that thing and enrich your knowledge. This is a personal choice, and also a collective choice that society needs to make. VII. Open Source or Closed Source? Where does the value of IP lie? This is a question that many entrepreneurs are concerned about, especially after the emergence of DeepSeek. Dario's point is that many open-source models from China are optimized for benchmark testing, and their performance in real-world scenarios isn't as good as the benchmark scores suggest. Researchers have designed a set of "unpublished benchmarks," and the performance of these models has significantly declined. This indicates that they were optimized for the "exam," but their real-world capabilities aren't as strong. However, his core judgment isn't about open-source versus closed-source, but about the power-law distribution of quality. He says the economics of AI models are very different from other technologies. Users have a strong preference for quality—just like hiring workers, the difference between the best programmer and the 10,000th best programmer is exponential, not linear. Under this logic, price isn't the most important variable; capability is. If a model is the best, users are willing to pay more for it and will prioritize it over a cheaper but slightly weaker alternative. Therefore, his strategy is simple: focus on making the smartest model. Everything else is secondary. One last question: What does Dario know that most people don't? His answer was surprisingly humble. He said his worldview could actually be derived from publicly available information; there were no secrets. However, he shared a repeatedly verified experience: When you use first principles to reason, combined with a small amount of empirical observation, you often arrive at counterintuitive conclusions—conclusions that almost no one believes, but which are ultimately proven correct. He said that over the past 10 years, he has repeatedly seen people say, "This can't happen, it's too strange, too much has changed, someone must be in charge." Then, those things happened one by one. This doesn't require inside information. It only requires a willingness to reason carefully and a willingness to accept counterintuitive conclusions. Key Conclusions Summary: Regarding technological assessments: The law of scale is true: Data × Computing Power × Model Scale = Intelligence. This equation is still under verification. AI models are very close to reaching human intelligence levels, but social cognition lags far behind. Research on the controllability of AI technology (interpretability, alignment) is progressing slightly better than expected. Regarding career advice: Programming will be taken over by AI first, software engineering as a whole will follow later; Human-centered work, physical world work, and critical thinking—these three directions have long-term value; Comparative advantage still exists; even if you only do 5%, your productivity can increase 20 times. Regarding AI Risks: Human skills can indeed deteriorate if AI is used incorrectly; The issue of AI consciousness is not science fiction, but is evolving into a real engineering and ethical problem; Critical thinking to distinguish between true and false content will be the core competitiveness in the AI ​​era.

data.title: Kraken's Direct Connection to the Federal Reserve: How to Usher in a New Era of Trillions of Dollars in Finance
data.description:
Author: The Giant Mirror of History

When the approval document containing hundreds of pages of compliance audits in the Federal Reserve Bank building was officially stamped, the traders on Wall Street and the geeks in Silicon Valley did not immediately realize that they had just witnessed a "fall of the Berlin Wall" in financial history.

On March 4th, after years of negotiations, cryptocurrency bank Kraken Financial finally received approval to open a master account with the Federal Reserve. This was not an ordinary license issuance. For the first time in nearly a century, the core payment infrastructure of the United States has opened its doors to a native digital asset depository institution. Cryptocurrency, once denounced as a "Ponzi scheme" by the CEO of JPMorgan Chase and relentlessly attacked by traditional banks in their de-banking movement, has finally planted a flag of compliance in the heart of the national credit system. But behind this historic milestone lies the collective fear of traditional banks, the Federal Reserve's perilous tightrope walk, the life-or-death battle between the two stablecoin giants, and the frenzy of trillions of dollars in Wall Street capital. To understand the significance of Kraken's breakthrough, we must first understand the moats of traditional finance. For a long time, crypto exchanges and stablecoin issuers have had to bow to traditional commercial banks (such as Chase Bank and Bank of New York Mellon) to handle US dollars. These commercial banks are a privileged class with master accounts at the Federal Reserve. They act as tollbooths, not only charging exorbitant fees but also wielding life-or-death power—a mere mention of compliance risk can instantly sever the fiat currency lifeline of a crypto company. Kraken Financial, as a Wyoming-licensed special purpose depository, received its master account approval, meaning it bypasses all commercial banks and establishes a direct link with the Federal Reserve's underlying dollar clearing network. However, the Federal Reserve is not embracing crypto unconditionally. In our in-depth investigation, Fed insiders described it as a "controlled stress test." Kraken received a special pass known as a "slimmed-down account." This account is essentially a VIP in shackles: Kraken cannot borrow money through the Federal Reserve's discount window like traditional banks, and is absolutely not allowed to overdraft even a single cent during daytime transfers. Hard Circuit Breaker Mechanism: The Federal Reserve has implemented stringent flow restrictions at the underlying technology level. In the event of an extreme run on the crypto market, similar to the FTX crash, the system will automatically block excess fiat currency outflows to prevent the liquidity black hole from spreading to national payment networks. Microscope-Level Penetration Audit: Kraken must provide API-level data penetration to prove that 100% of the USD it absorbs from customers is held in full by the Federal Reserve and is not used for any high-risk collateral or lending. The Federal Reserve's calculations are shrewd. Rather than letting hundreds of billions of dollars in cryptocurrency roam freely in the uncontrolled offshore "shadow banking" system, it's better to confine it within a specially crafted, completely transparent cage of its own making. The banking industry's fury: The cash cow that has been taken away. Less than 24 hours after the approval was announced, lobbying groups on K Street in Washington were in an uproar. The American Bankers Association and the Banking Policy Institute issued their strongest warning letter in recent years. On the surface, they waved the banner of preventing systemic risk, accusing allowing an institution without federal deposit insurance backing to access the core payment system of inviting trouble. But beneath the veneer of compliance lies a complete subversion of the distribution of benefits. This is tantamount to regulatory arbitrage. An executive from a major Wall Street bank complained to me anonymously in an interview, "We pay hundreds of millions of dollars in FDIC premiums every year and maintain a huge, bloated compliance team to meet the Basel Accords' capital adequacy ratios. And Kraken only needs to act as a simple, fully-reserve channel to use our payment infrastructure on equal footing? Why?" Even more fatal is the disintermediation. Commercial banks have long relied on monopolizing dollar settlement channels, risk-free profiting from the massive amounts of idle funds held by crypto institutions while charging exorbitant tolls. With Kraken's channel open, not only exchanges, but also stablecoin issuers (like Circle) if they follow this path, will forever lose this lucrative cash cow. The banking industry's anger stems from a deep fear of becoming a low-profit, bottom-tier conduit. The Ultimate Battle for Stablecoin Supremacy: The Birth of a Synthetic Digital Dollar If traditional banks are experiencing growing pains, then for the stablecoin sector, Kraken has brought a devastating nuclear explosion. It will directly determine the benchmark pricing power of cryptocurrencies for the next decade. This has triggered a major battle between Circle, the parent company of USDC, and Tether, the parent company of USDT. USDC's Decisive Strike: Becoming a Synthetic CBDC Recall the weekend of 2023 when Silicon Valley Bank collapsed, USDC plummeted to $0.87 due to $3.3 billion in trapped reserves—the darkest moment for compliant stablecoins—their fatal weakness lay in counterparty risk. But now, Kraken provides a perfect template. If Circle's dollar reserves could be directly deposited into the Federal Reserve's main account, USDC's credit rating would undergo a qualitative leap, directly anchoring to the US national credit. This will bring two disruptive consequences: Wall Street institutions will no longer need to worry about the bankruptcy of underlying banks when conducting on-chain transactions worth tens of billions of dollars.

  • Squeezing every last drop of interest from the Federal Reserve：By bypassing commercial banks, compliant stablecoin issuers can directly earn interest on the Federal Reserve's reserves. If Circle launches compliant interest-bearing USDC in the future, passing on the risk-free benchmark interest rate (e.g., 4%) to on-chain users, it will be a fatal drain on funds from the entire crypto market.

    • Tether's Wandering Earth Plan

    Faced with this almost superhuman, national-level disruptive attack, Tether will not sit idly by. Due to its complex offshore structure and lack of US regulation, Tether will never obtain a ticket to the Federal Reserve's main account. Tether's counter-strategy is extremely decisive: complete decentralization from the US and a shift towards the global South. It will no longer try to appease institutional funds on Wall Street, but will instead fully transform into the "national digital cash" for high-inflation countries in Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia. Simultaneously, Tether will further reduce its reliance on US Treasury bonds in its reserves, instead increasing its holdings of gold and Bitcoin. The fiat currency anchor in the crypto world will be officially split into the onshore compliant US dollar (USDC) and the offshore shadow dollar (USDT). The Trillion-Dollar Playground of Wall Street: The Core Engine of RWA is Ignited. Why are giants like BlackRock and Fidelity, who manage trillions of dollars, so excited about Kraken's approval? Because it completes the most crucial piece of the RWA puzzle: perfect DVP (Deliverable Versus Payment). In traditional financial markets, when you buy a US Treasury bond, there's a time lag between the asset transfer and fiat currency settlement (usually T+1 or T+2), during which significant settlement risks are hidden. When Federal Reserve-level stablecoins emerged, a miracle occurred: Tokenized assets (such as BlackRock's BUIDL fund) and digital dollars could be locked simultaneously in the same smart contract. Once the transaction conditions were triggered, assets and funds were atomically exchanged within a millionth of a second. No commercial bank could interfere, and no clearinghouse would shut down for the weekend. This 24/7, zero-counter-risk, programmable underlying clearing network would act like a black hole, frantically sucking traditional repurchase markets, private equity, and money market funds into the blockchain world. DeFi would officially transform into OnFi (On-Chain Finance) and gain a real, risk-free benchmark interest rate from the Federal Reserve. The Three Kingdoms of Public Chains: Ethereum, Solana, and Bitcoin's Path to Success Strategies Tens of trillions of dollars of traditional capital are preparing to enter the market, but on which public chain should they anchor? The Kraken incident directly catalyzed the fierce competition for underlying infrastructure. 1. Ethereum: Wall Street's Old Money Club and Liquidity Concerns For traditional asset management giants who are extremely risk-averse, the Ethereum mainnet is currently the only time-tested and sufficiently decentralized settlement layer. However, Ethereum's Rollup-centric scaling approach has fragmented the ecosystem into numerous liquidity silos (Arbitrum, Base, etc.). Wall Street demands globally unified, instant settlement; cross-chain latency and bridging vulnerabilities are the biggest Achilles' heel of the Ethereum ecosystem, currently swallowing trillions of dollars in RWA funds. 2. Solana: A Nasdaq-Level Financial High-Frequency Engine Solana's monolithic architecture demonstrates remarkable suitability at this moment. Its ultra-low latency, high concurrency, and globally unified state make the clearing of extremely complex financial derivatives and instant RWA swaps possible. Coupled with its underlying token expansion functionality, allowing institutions to directly set compliance thresholds at the protocol layer (such as transfer whitelists), Solana is striving to become the Nasdaq of the Web3 era. 3. The Bitcoin Ecosystem: The Dark Web King's Counterattack Don't forget Tether, forced to retreat. To escape potential US regulatory scrutiny of Ethereum or Solana nodes, USDT will migrate en masse to the most censorship-resistant Bitcoin network. This will enable extremely low-cost P2P payments through the Lightning Network and absolute transaction anonymity through client verification protocols like RGB. The Bitcoin ecosystem will support a parallel world financial network completely unaffected by the Federal Reserve's oversight. Standing in the spring of 2026, through the crack torn open by Kraken, we see a completely different future. The future financial world will be folded. On the top floor, bathed in sunlight, are Wall Street bankers in suits. Using digital dollars pegged to the Federal Reserve, they instantly clear billions of dollars in US debt on Solana or Ethereum—all efficiently, compliantly, and transparently. At the bottom of the cyberpunk world are Argentine workers and Nigerian businessmen. They use the Bitcoin Lightning Network on their phones to send and receive Tether shadow dollars, which are absolutely censorship-resistant, to combat their countries' hyperinflation. Kraken obtained a ticket to the Federal Reserve, but it opened up a vast journey between two parallel financial universes.

data.title: Bitcoin faces test from the Iranian crisis: Is it a safe-haven asset or just a coincidence?
data.description:
Author: James Butterfill, Head of Research, CoinShares; Source: CoinShares blog; Compiled by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

As the Iranian geopolitical crisis reshapes global risk appetite, Bitcoin's price movements and fund flow data are telling a story that investors can no longer ignore.

Geopolitics returned to the center of the market over the weekend, with US President Trump once again choosing to take significant action on Saturday, triggering market turmoil. The evacuation of British embassy personnel had previously served as a warning, so the market was not entirely caught off guard, but the rapid developments afterwards forced us to re-examine the position of digital assets in the modern safe-haven asset system.

The Strait of Hormuz: Why This Crisis Is Different The key question is not just whether Iran can be contained, but how quickly any disruptive event can spread through the global economy. Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 21% of global oil trade passes daily. This is not a peripheral chokepoint, but one of the few geographical features upon which the global energy supply system truly depends. This crisis has moved from rhetoric to concrete action. The withdrawal of marine insurance business and signs of significant tanker congestion in the region indicate that those involved in shipping transactions are making concrete decisions, not just standing by. Drone activity, coupled with signals of a renewed alliance between Hezbollah and the Houthis, has significantly increased the likelihood of the conflict spreading beyond the bilateral confrontation. The 13% volatility in the oil market directly reflects this vulnerability, while gold's current 1.8% decline may reflect more its recent overvaluation than the impact of the geopolitical crisis.

Compared to the price movements of oil or gold, Bitcoin's price action has been more captivating. As the only major liquid asset that continues to trade over the weekend, Bitcoin has historically played a role in forcing investors to reduce their risk exposure when traditional markets are closed. In past weekend shocks, whether geopolitical or macroeconomic, Bitcoin has typically absorbed selling pressure and rebounded after the stock market reopened and risk redistributed.

This time, things are different. Bitcoin's rise did not trigger broader market turmoil. Instead of becoming a tool for panic selling, it attracted funds. This contrast is significant.

Understanding Bitcoin's resilience requires considering the specific context. In the five months prior to this price surge, we estimate that net outflows from whales totaled approximately $30 billion, with these large holders continuously selling, pushing the price down to key technical support levels and valuation bottoms. This extended sell-off period is crucial because it significantly altered the marginal supply situation. When geopolitical risks re-emerged, the market had already absorbed a significant amount of aggressive selling. The Market Cap/Real Value (MVRV) valuation has compressed to approximately one standard deviation below real value, a level historically associated with the later stages of a correction rather than its continuation. Bitcoin's Relative Strength Index (RSI) recently hit a low of 16, indicating an extremely oversold market by any historical standard. **Leverage ratios have fallen from 33% in October 2025 to 25%, returning to the long-term average, meaning that excess speculative funds have been largely cleared from the system.** In short, **by the time the Iranian crisis erupted, the market had already completed most of its self-adjustment. The market structure is clearer, marginal sellers have decreased, and marginal buyers are clearly prepared.** Fund flow data provides key evidence: after five consecutive weeks of net outflows from ETFs totaling $4.3 billion (comparable to the peak outflows during previous corrections), **last week saw a net inflow of $1 billion.** On Monday alone, the first trading day after the weekend's escalation, $500 million inflows were recorded. Investors are not fleeing the market; they are pouring in.** However, the situation is not without its challenges. The latest Producer Price Index (PPI) data far exceeded expectations, rising 0.5% month-on-month, higher than the previously predicted 0.3%, with the core PPI at 0.8%, mainly driven by trade and services. Given the current surge in energy prices triggered by the situation in Iran, commodity inflation may also rise. The consequences are predictable: expectations of interest rate cuts have been further postponed, with the futures market currently viewing the probability of a June rate cut as less than 50%. For traditional risk assets, the impact is obvious: a prolonged tightening of financial conditions negatively affects stock and credit markets. For Bitcoin, the situation is more complex. In the short term, a high-interest-rate environment reduces the attractiveness of non-yielding assets. However, as the tension between energy-driven inflation and central bank credibility persists, Bitcoin's advantage as a non-sovereign scarce asset becomes increasingly apparent.

Mid-term Outlook: Could the Strait of Hormuz Be a Catalyst for Bitcoin?

Imagine a scenario that is at least possible: a prolonged disruption of the Strait of Hormuz shipping lanes, leading to soaring energy prices, strained supply chains, and potential fiscal pressure on energy-importing countries. In this scenario, the reliability of the global financial system, correspondent banks, dollar settlement infrastructure, and the smooth operation of international trade finance are no longer taken for granted, but become real variables.

It is in this environment that Bitcoin's structural characteristics—non-sovereign issuance, independence of bearer settlement, and fixed supply—can translate from theoretical advantages into practical advantages. The freezing of approximately $300 billion in reserves held by the Central Bank of Russia in 2022 demonstrates that high-quality sovereign assets held abroad can become unavailable overnight, not due to default, but because custodian institutions and settlement infrastructure exercised their jurisdiction.

If the Strait of Hormuz crisis continues, it will test the resilience of another set of financial assumptions, and the asset classes most likely to benefit from such pressure are those that have no issuers, no counterparties, and are not dependent on the troubled infrastructure. For investors evaluating this investment logic, the following indicators are most valuable in the short term: Sustained ETF inflows; a reversal of five consecutive weeks of outflows would represent a significant shift in market sentiment; Whale Wallet activity to determine if fund allocation has truly eased between October 2025 and February 2026; Energy price trends and their secondary impact on inflation expectations and the timing of interest rate cuts; Will the disruption to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz further exacerbate the existing congestion issues for insurance companies and oil tankers? Investment Perspective: Our fundamental expectation remains short-term market consolidation, slightly biased towards the downside. The macroeconomic environment is not entirely favorable, and escalating geopolitical uncertainty has a dual impact on risk appetite. However, the situation is changing. With leverage rebalancing, a clear stabilization in whale holdings, valuation metrics returning to normal, and the crucial $1.5 billion ETF inflow coinciding with heightened geopolitical risks, the market is exhibiting the characteristics of a mature safe-haven asset. Maintaining this structure weekly further enhances its advantages. The Iranian crisis has not given rise to the safe-haven asset argument for Bitcoin. However, it provides the clearest real-world test of this argument in the current cycle. Looking at the price action over the past 72 hours, Bitcoin is undergoing this test.

data.title: NYSE parent company invests in OKX Revolut, which is applying for a U.S. banking license.
data.description:
Headline

▌Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), invests in cryptocurrency exchange OKX. This investment values ​​OKX at a staggering $25 billion, marking a significant move by traditional financial giants to aggressively enter the blockchain space and drive the development of blockchain-based stock trading. Under the agreement, ICE will gain a seat on OKX's board of directors. The two institutions will launch several collaborative products, including OKX providing ICE with real-time cryptocurrency price information, and OKX users will be able to trade tokenized stocks and derivatives listed on the NYSE on the platform, a feature expected to launch in the second half of 2026. OKX also plans to relocate up to 2,000 employees (40%) to the United States to strengthen its domestic operations and support new products.

... According to market sources, British digital banking giant Revolut has officially submitted an application seeking a US banking license. This move aims to further expand its business footprint and market influence within the US financial system. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $70,942.73, a 24-hour change of -2.4%; ETH price is $2,077.11, a 24-hour change of -1.9%; BNB price is $648.12, a 24-hour change of -1.6%. The price of SOL is $88.90, a 24-hour change of -1.9%; the price of DOGE is $0.09364, a 24-hour change of -5.5%; the price of XRP is $1.41, a 24-hour change of -1.2%; and the price of TRX is $0.2853, a 24-hour change of -0.6%. WLFI price is $0.1028, a 24-hour change of -3.7%; HYPE price is $30.58, a 24-hour change of -4.8%. Policy: ▌US SEC Advances Clarification on the Applicability of Crypto Asset Securities Law. According to market news: The US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has submitted interpretative guidance to the Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (OIRA) for interagency review. After the review, three SEC commissioners will vote on it. According to foreign media citing sources, US officials have drafted legislation to restrict the global shipment of artificial intelligence chips not approved by the US. The bill would grant Washington broad powers to determine whether and under what conditions other countries can build facilities for training and running AI models. The proposed regulations would require companies to apply for US licenses to export almost all AI accelerators produced by companies such as Nvidia and AMD. This would extend the current controls, which cover approximately 40 countries, to a global scale. President Trump has repeatedly stated that he wants the world to use American AI, and the draft legislation is not intended to ban Nvidia exports. Instead, the legislation would designate the US government as the "gatekeeper" for the AI ​​industry: companies (and in some cases, their home governments) would have to obtain approval from the US Department of Commerce to purchase accelerators.

▌Trump: Must Personally Involve in Iran's Succession Arrangements, Will Not Accept Khamenei's Son as Supreme Leader

According to Axios, President Trump stated in an interview on Thursday that he needs to personally participate in the selection of Iran's next leader, just as he did in Venezuela. Trump acknowledged that Mojtaba Khamenei, the son of the assassinated Supreme Leader Khamenei, is the most likely successor, but also made it clear that he considers this outcome unacceptable. For several days, the Iranian regime has delayed announcing its successor to the Supreme Leader. However, statements from Iranian political figures on Thursday suggested that an announcement may be imminent. Trump said, "They are wasting their time. Khamenei's son is an insignificant person. I must participate in the appointment process as I did with Delcy Rodriguez in Venezuela." He added that he rejects any new Iranian leader who would continue Khamenei's policies, which would force the United States to return to war "within five years."

...

Blockchain Applications

▌Coinbase CEO and Other Executives Sued by Shareholders for False Disclosure and Compliance Negligence

Coinbase shareholder Kevin Meehan has filed a derivative lawsuit against CEO Brian Armstrong and several other executives, accusing them of making false statements between April 2021 and June 2023, concealing the risk that escrowed assets might be considered company property in the event of bankruptcy, and alleging that some of the tokens launched may constitute securities. The lawsuit also alleges “widespread and long-standing deficiencies” in Coinbase’s anti-money laundering mechanisms and mentions a $500 million fine imposed by the New York Department of Financial Services in 2023 and previous enforcement actions by the SEC. The lawsuit also accuses executives of using insider information to sell shares during the IPO period. Consensys senior attorney Bill Hughes pointed out in a post on X that because the case is structured as a shareholder derivative lawsuit, any monetary damages will go to Coinbase, not directly to the shareholders.

▌Seven Major US Tech Giants Sign Self-Powering Pledge

According to CCTV Finance, on the 4th local time, representatives from seven companies—Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, Amazon, Meta, xAI, and Oracle—signed a document at the White House. US President Trump stated that many Americans are concerned that data centers will drive up electricity demand, potentially leading to higher electricity bills. This document will address that concern. According to previous forecasts by the International Energy Agency, global data center electricity demand will more than double by 2030, reaching approximately 945 terawatt-hours. Reports indicate that due to surging demand, the US's ambitious expansion plans for the artificial intelligence industry may be severely hampered by bottlenecks in power infrastructure, including turbine shortages and slow grid expansion.

... OpenAI has officially released GPT-5.4, offering a "Thinking" mode, and launching it on ChatGPT, API, and Codex platforms. GPT-5.4 Pro, optimized for complex tasks, was also released. OpenAI states that in ChatGPT, GPT-5.4's "Thinking" mode is improved for deep web research, with better context preservation during long-term thinking—users can now interrupt the model's response to add instructions or adjust its direction. The guided feature is available on Android and web this week, with an iOS version coming soon. OpenAI developers say GPT-5.4 has native computing capabilities. Codex and API support up to 1 million context tags, enabling optimal proxy coding for complex tasks, providing scalable tool search across a larger ecosystem, and enabling more efficient inference for long, tool-intensive workflows.

▌Neura Robotics Plans to Raise €1 Billion, Tether May Participate

German robotics startup Neura Robotics is preparing a new round of financing of approximately €1 billion to accelerate the development of humanoid robots driven by artificial intelligence. Sources familiar with the matter say that Tether, the issuer of the stablecoin USDT, is expected to participate in this round of investment. This round of financing may value the Metzingen, Germany-based Neura Robotics at approximately €4 billion, and further financing may follow. Founded in 2019 by David Reger, Neura Robotics focuses on developing "cognitive robots" with visual, auditory, and environmental perception capabilities.

... Cryptocurrency

According to the latest disclosure, Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group (MUFG), with assets of $3 trillion, announced two strategic advancements: Deep integration of AI into the credit system: Generative AI will be fully applied to the loan review process for businesses and individuals, significantly improving risk control accuracy and approval efficiency; Stablecoin proof-of-concept implementation: In collaboration with Mitsubishi Bank and Sumitomo Mitsui Bank, MUFG is advancing the proof-of-concept (PoC) of a yen-pegged stablecoin, paving the way for its official commercial launch in March 2026.

OKX Star: Plans to Connect 120 Million Users to ICE Futures and NYSE Tokenized Stock Markets According to official news, OKX founder and CEO Stephen Star stated that Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) has completed its investment in OKX and will join the company's board of directors. ICE owns the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and a global derivatives and clearing platform. This collaboration reflects the consensus between the two parties that digital asset technology will play a long-term role in the future financial markets. Star pointed out that OKX plans to provide its more than 120 million global users with access to the ICE US futures market and the NYSE tokenized stock market, exploring the integration path of traditional financial markets and digital asset infrastructure within a compliant framework. Star also stated that as blockchain and artificial intelligence technologies drive structural changes in financial markets, the two parties will collaborate on tokenized securities and the digitization of traditional assets to explore more efficient and transparent next-generation financial infrastructure models.

▌Bitwise Donates Over $383,000 to Support Bitcoin Ecosystem

Asset management company Bitwise announced that it has donated a total of $383,000 to Bitcoin development projects since 2024. The latest donation of $233,000 is the second annual action fulfilling its commitment to "use 10% of the gross profit of the Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB) to support open-source developers." The funds will be distributed through Brink, OpenSats, and the HRF Bitcoin Development Fund to ensure the security and continued innovation of the Bitcoin network.

▌CryptoQuant CEO: MARA Discloses Bitcoin Mining Costs Rise to $70,027 Per Coin

According to Ki Young Ju, founder and CEO of CryptoQuant, based on the latest filing with MARA, the current cost of Bitcoin mining has reached $70,027 per coin.

The Smarter Web Company ($SWC), a UK-based Bitcoin treasury company, has announced that it will be officially included in the FTSE UK Index Series on March 23, becoming a constituent stock of the FTSE All-Share and FTSE SmallCap indices. This signifies that publicly traded companies holding Bitcoin are gaining increasing recognition from traditional capital markets.

▌Analyst: Stable Dividends to Attract Significant Capital Inflow as Strategy Preferred Stock STRC Marks One-Year Anniversary

Bitcoin analyst "Grain of Salt" (@Z06Z07) points out that market attention to the perpetual preferred stock $STRC issued by Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) will significantly increase as the one-year anniversary approaches.

▌Coinbase Launches OPINION (OPN)

Coinbase announced on its X platform that OPINION (OPN) is now available on its website and app, allowing users to buy, sell, convert, send, receive, or store OPN assets.

**Important Economic Developments** **▌Secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council: Prepared for US Ground Action** **On May 5th, Larijani, Secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, posted on social media that Iran is prepared for a US ground operation. He stated that Iran is well aware of the plans of some US officials to launch a ground operation in Iran, but Iran is prepared to "shame those US officials" at any cost. (CCTV News)** **▌US Federal Judge Rules Trump's Tariffs Must Be Fully Refunded** **According to market news: The US Federal Trade Court has ruled that all importers are entitled to a refund of tariffs previously overturned by the Supreme Court. This could result in the return of up to $175 billion, undoubtedly a major blow to the current administration.**

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in March is 97.3%

According to CME's "FedWatch": The probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by 25 basis points by March is 2.7%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 97.3%. The probability of the Fed cutting interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by April is 12.5%, the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 87.3%, and the probability of cutting interest rates by a cumulative 50 basis points is 0.3%. The probability of cutting interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by June is 30.7%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌What is the value of BTC?

BTC is often compared to "digital gold," but its core value is actually the "promise of immutable scarcity."
